scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lysias was an orator in 4th-century Athens; the 'corn-controllers' (sitophylakes) were specific Athenian magistrates.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech Against the Corn-Dealers is traditionally dated to 386 BCE, a time of grain scarcity in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The eisphora (special levy) was a direct tax on wealthy residents of the Athenian city-state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech reflects Athenian fiscal pressure and the enforcement of the eisphora circa 386 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The orator addresses an Athenian audience regarding their collective power to vote in the Assembly or Law Courts.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The prosecution of Epicrates for his failed embassy occurred around 390 BCE after the Corinthian War began.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The triobolon (three-obol payment) for jury service was a distinctive feature of the Radical Democracy in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech mentions the daily stipend as a contemporary reality for the jurors at the trial of Epicrates, c. 390 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian law strictly regulated the conduct of envoys (presbeis) through the process of euthyna (audit) and potential capital trials.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The trial of Epicrates and his fellow envoys for corruption took place in roughly 390 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The choregia (funding of dramas) was one of the primary liturgies imposed on the wealthy in Classical Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The orator describes the performance of services like producing dramas as current obligations during the trial of Epicrates, c. 390 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The right to sentence public officials to death for misappropriating public funds was a power of the Athenian dikasterion.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The trial of Philocrates followed that of Ergocles, occurring roughly in 388 BCE after the Thrasybulus expedition.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The trierarchy (equipping warships) was the most expensive liturgy for wealthy Athenian citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lysias describes the burden of equipping warships during the naval campaigns of the late 390s/early 380s BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Failure to pay the eisphora tax could lead to severe penalties, including the confiscation of the debtor's estate by the Athenian state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The speech Against Philocrates refers to contemporary confiscation practices around 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian law held many types of accomplices to the same standard as principals, especially in crimes against the state treasury.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule is cited during the prosecution of Philocrates, the associate of Ergocles, in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Annual audits (euthynai) were mandatory for all Athenian magistrates; Nicomachus was a public clerk (anagrapheus) under this law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This speech from 399 BCE attacks Nicomachus for failing to submit to an audit after his extended tenure as a law-coder.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian law sought to prevent the accumulation of power by prohibiting same-office repeats for under-clerks (hypogrammateis).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lysias cites this specific rotation rule as a contrast to Nicomachus's long-term hold on power in 399 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Public slaves (demosioi) in Athens worked in administration but were legally incapable of owning the state's property.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The orator uses Nicomachus's alleged humble or servile origins to insult him during the 399 BCE trial.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The protection and funding of 'ancestral sacrifices' (patria) was a core legislative concern of the Athenian people and their calendar.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: In 399 BCE, the revision of the laws including the sacral calendar was a major political issue in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: In Athens, persons falsely claiming citizenship (xenias graphe) who were proven to be of slave birth could be sold as slaves by the state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rhetorical threat of sale reflects Athenian status-boundary laws current in 399 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The dikasteria (courts) composed of citizen-jurors held the ultimate sovereign right to punish those who violated or subverted the city's laws.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lysias frames this judicial power for the jury during the prosecution of Nicomachus in 399 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The practice of 'paraklesis' (summoning friends to speak in one's support) was a standard procedural right in Athenian trials.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The orator anticipates the traditional use of character witnesses or intercessors at the trial in 399 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes Jewish ritual law (animal sacrifice) through a Christian polemical lens during the Roman period.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Epistle of Barnabas is generally dated to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE; 100 CE is a representative midpoint.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author argues that Jews have surrendered their legal and religious standing as the 'covenant' people due to past transgressions.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text reflects early Christian supersessionist views regarding the Jewish covenant status around 100 CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The author is describing the 'new law' governing the burgeoning Christian community as distinct from Jewish ritual requirements.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The author's floruit and the internal evidence of the letter place this theological 'rule' around 100 CE.
